explanation: The input states that whenever the robot does not take the optimal solution, an explanation must be provided. This is best captured by an LTL property where globally (at all times), if 'notOptimal' is true (the chosen solution is not optimal), then 'explain' (an explanation is provided) must also be true. Thus, the correct LTL property is G(notOptimal -> explain). The previously given LTL properties (none are given) may not capture this precisely if, for example, they used eventuality (F) instead of implication or failed to globally enforce the requirement.

The requirement states that whenever the robot does not take the optimal solution (represented by 'notOptimal'), there must be an explanation provided (represented by 'explain'). The correct LTL formula for this is G(notOptimal -> explain), meaning that globally, anytime 'notOptimal' holds, 'explain' must also hold at that moment (since the explanation should be given whenever the solution is not optimal).

The natural language states that whenever the solution taken is not optimal, an explanation must be provided. In LTL, this is captured by the formula G(notOptimal -> explain), which requires that on all paths and at all times, if notOptimal holds, explain must also hold. This ensures that the requirement is continuously enforced and explanations are given whenever a non-optimal solution is chosen.

The input specifies that an explanation is required whenever the robot does not take the optimal solution. In other words, whenever the condition 'notOptimal' holds (indicating a suboptimal choice), the system should ensure that an 'explain' action follows. In LTL, this is most directly expressed as 'Globally: if notOptimal then eventually explain', i.e., G(notOptimal -> F explain). This guarantees that for any point where a suboptimal solution is taken, an explanation will be provided in the future. This formulation precisely captures the requirement stated in the natural language.

The requirement is that whenever the robot does not take the optimal solution (indicated by 'notOptimal'), it should provide an explanation ('explain'). In LTL, this means whenever 'notOptimal' is true, then 'explain' must also be true at that moment (globally, 'notOptimal' implies 'explain'). This directly captures the intent from the natural language: an explanation is needed whenever a non-optimal solution is chosen.

This formula specifies that whenever the chosen solution is not optimal (notOptimal), an explanation (explain) must be provided at the same time or afterwards. This captures the requirement that if a non-optimal solution is taken, there must be an explanation for it. The formula F(notOptimal & F(explain)) would not be sufficient, because it only requires that there eventually exists a time where both notOptimal and (eventually) explain hold, not that every instance of notOptimal leads to an explanation. The given formula G(notOptimal -> F(explain)) ensures that for every occurrence of notOptimal, an explanation is eventually provided.

The requirement states that whenever the robot does not take the optimal solution (notOptimal), an explanation must be provided (explain). In LTL, this is best expressed by stating that globally (G), whenever notOptimal holds, explain must also hold, typically written as G(notOptimal -> explain). This ensures that every occurrence of a non-optimal choice is accompanied by an explanation. Any other LTL formulation (such as only occasionally providing an explanation or requiring it in advance) would not guarantee that an explanation occurs exactly when needed according to the requirement.

The requirement states that an explanation must be provided whenever the chosen solution is not optimal. In LTL, this means that at all times, if 'notOptimal' holds, then 'explain' must also hold—formally, it is always the case that 'notOptimal' implies 'explain'. This is captured by the formula G (notOptimal -> explain).

The requirement states that whenever the robot does not take the optimal solution, it must provide an explanation. In LTL, this is captured by the property: globally, if notOptimal is true, then explain must also be true (either immediately or at the same time). This ensures that an explanation always accompanies or follows a non-optimal decision. There are no prior LTL properties to compare. The formula 'G(notOptimal -> explain)' expresses this directly: globally, whenever notOptimal holds, explain must also hold.

The specification requires that whenever the robot does not take the optimal solution (notOptimal), it must provide an explanation (explain). In LTL, this is most typically captured by saying that globally, whenever 'notOptimal' becomes true, eventually 'explain' will also become true. This directly matches the stated requirement: when (if ever) the robot deviates from optimality, an explanation must follow. The LTL property G (notOptimal -> F explain) expresses this condition precisely. Previous LTL properties not using this pattern might fail to capture the causality and temporal relationship expressed in the natural language requirement.
